Title: Engineering Malaysia's new energy transition
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In industrial facilities, steam energy systems often work quietly behind the scenes, ensuring operations run efficiently and reliably every day.
Their vital role may go unnoticed, yet their performance underpins productivity, cost efficiency and compliance.
This essential responsibility has profoundly shaped See Hooi Nee's 17-year career as senior design manager at Wasco Greenergy Bhd.
Today, she leads the design of biomass steam energy systems that deliver continuous, high-pressure steam to industrial users while supporting Malaysia's renewable energy ambitions and advancing decarbonisation initiatives through biomass utilisation.
As International Women's Day 2026 highlights the importance of greater representation in leadership across industries, See's role illustrates how women are increasingly shaping technical leadership in sectors critical to the energy transition.
Together with World Engineering Day for Sustainable Development, her work reflects how engineering expertise and accountability are driving the infrastructure behind a more sustainable future.
Over nearly two decades, she has worked on projects ranging from two tonnes per hour to 90 tonnes per hour, with pressure designs from 10 barg up to 85 barg.
This extensive scale highlights not only the technical complexity of her work but also the immense operational responsibility she holds in facilities where reliability is paramount.
In heavy industrial engineering, leadership roles have traditionally been dominated by men.
As more women step into technical leadership roles, professionals like See reflect a gradual shift in how engineering leadership is evolving.
Designing For Long-Term Performance Malaysia's palm oil industry generates substantial residues such as empty fruit bunches, palm kernel shells and palm oil mill effluent.
These materials power biomass steam energy systems that support industrial operations while lowering reliance on fossil fuels.
For See, the significance of her role became clear early in her career.
One defining project was a 12-tonne single-pass boiler supplied to Kenya, undertaken while she was still young engineer.
"At that time, I was still very new, and taking on the project felt overwhelming," she recalled.
The assignment required her to scrutinise every calculation, validate every assumption and ultimately sign off on a system that would operate in an industrial environment thousands of kilometres away.
That experience reinforced her sense of responsibility and accountability.
"It is no longer enough for an engineer to deliver a system that simply works; we must think about how our solutions affect people, communities, and the environment, not just today, but for generations to come," she said.
This philosophy now guides her approach to every project.
Designing for optimal performance means managing variability.
Biomass, for instance, can vary in moisture content and combustion behaviour, requiring system to remain stable and efficient under different conditions.
See ensures her designs do just that.
"Instead of letting valuable energy escape through the chimney, we look for ways to recover that heat using economisers and air preheaters. This recovered heat can then be used to support other parts of the process," she explained.
As Wasco Greenergy expand beyond a traditional EPCC-driven model into long-term asset ownership under BOOT and BOO frameworks, its role becomes even more critical.
By owning, operating and maintaining biomass steam energy systems over extended periods, the company takes on greater responsibility for how these systems perform over time.
This places stronger emphasis on engineering decisions that drive long-term efficiency, reliability and cost discipline.
Engineering Responsibility in Action
While the industry is evolving, it remains a demanding environment.
For See, navigating it has required both technical discipline and personal resilience.
"For me, it means working twice as hard and making sure you never stumble over the same stone twice," she said.
"You stay prepared, stay sharp and and stand firm in rooms where people may underestimate you before they understand your work."
Over time, results speak louder than assumptions.
"Every project completed and every challenge solved is proof of something powerful," she added. "We are never out of place. Follow your passion, show your grit, and own where you belong."
See's journey reflects the quiet determination behind many engineering breakthroughs.
In industries where precision and accountability matter every day, progress is shaped by individuals who combine technical expertise with a deep sense of responsibility.
As Malaysia accelerates its energy transition ambitions, the nation's progress hinges on engineers like See, who possess the unique ability to translate strategic visions into tangible, operating infrastructure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Firms urged to adopt flexible work arrangements amid global economic pressures
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Private sector employers are encouraged to adopt flexible work arrangements (FWA), including working from home, in line with amendments to the Employment Act 1955 that came into force on Jan 1, 2023.
The Human Resources Ministry said the amendments allow employees to apply for flexible working arrangements, strengthening modern workplace practices that are more responsive to current needs.
"These provisions allow employees to submit applications to employers for flexible arrangements in terms of working hours, working days, or place of work, including working from home," the ministry said in a statement today.
The
call
follows Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's push for wider adoption of flexible work practices, after the government expanded such arrangements within the civil service.
Meanwhile, the ministry said employers adopting FWA must not reduce statutory benefits provided under the Act, including overtime pay, rest day pay, public holiday pay, annual leave and sick leave.
It said FWA offers benefits to both employers and employees, including optimising resource utilisation and enhancing employee well-being through improved work-life balance.
"Private sector employers are encouraged to assess the suitability of implementing FWA within their respective organisations based on operational needs, including approaches such as working from home, flexible working hours, shift rotations or hybrid models," it said.
To support implementation, the ministry, through the Department of Labour of Peninsular Malaysia and TalentCorp, provides advisory services on policy development, training, and the sharing of best practices.
Employers may also obtain further information and participate in related FWA initiatives by contacting TalentCorp via email at flexworklife@talentcorp.com.my or by visiting www.flexworklife.my.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four homes gutted in Batu Ferringhi fire, access and water issues hamper operations
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Firefighters battling a blaze that destroyed four double-storey semi-permanent houses in Kampung Din Manbo, Batu Ferringhi, today faced significant challenges due to narrow access routes and low water pressure.
The difficult terrain and limited water supply hampered efforts to contain the fire, which sent a plume of thick black smoke billowing into the sky, visible from several kilometres away.
Despite the constraints, the Penang Fire and Rescue Department responded swiftly after receiving an emergency call at 11.29am.
Additional assistance was deployed from Teluk Bahang, Jalan Perak and Bayan Baru stations to support firefighting operations.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director John Sagun Francis said the fire involved four semi-permanent houses measuring about 40 by 50 square feet each.
"Initial reports indicated that about 80 per cent of the structures were destroyed in the fire.
"No casualties have been reported so far," he said.
He said firefighters managed to bring the blaze under control at 12.21pm, although operations were still ongoing at press time.
A total of 23 personnel, supported by multiple fire engines and units, were deployed to the scene, with police also present to assist in the operation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: VS Industry sinks to lowest since 2021 share split
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: VS Industry Bhd shares sank to a near five-year low after the electronics manufacturing services group slipped into losses, triggering heavy selling that pushed the counter to the top of Bursa Malaysia's most active list.
The stock fell as much as five sen or 17.54 per cent to 23.5 sen, its lowest level since the company's 2-for-1 share split in May 2021, before settling at 24 sen at midday, down 4.5 sen or 15.79 per cent.
The split adjusted its share price from RM2.64 to RM1.29.
As at 12.30pm, 128.26 million shares had changed hands, about four times its 200-day average, the counter's busiest session since Oct 3, 2025.
At 24 sen, VS Industry's market capitalisation stood at about RM947.45 million. The stock has nearly halved from 46.5 sen at the start of the year, amid a broader decline.
The group posted a net loss of RM29.59 million for the second quarter ended Jan 31, reversing from a net profit of RM15.38 million a year earlier.
It attributed the loss to weaker orders from key customers amid soft global consumer demand, which weighed on capacity utilisation, alongside ongoing cost optimisation efforts by clients.
Revenue fell 15.32 per cent to RM769.53 million from RM908.8 million previously.
The latest result follows a net loss of RM33.04 million recorded in the fourth quarter of the financial year ended July 31, 2025, on revenue of RM858.79 million.
According to MyBursa, at least 10 research houses cover the stock, with two "Strong Buy", four "Buy" and four "Hold" calls.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: George Kent issues RM80mil sukuk to strengthen capital structure
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: George Kent (Malaysia) Bhd has issued RM80 million in nominal value sukuk under its sukuk wakalah programme, as part of its ongoing efforts to optimise its capital structure.
The issuance was completed in accordance with the terms of the programme and forms part of the company's strategy to maintaini a balanced and sustainable capital structure.
The company said the sukuk reflects its continued access to the capital markets.
"George Kent remains focused on prudent capital management and will continue to evaluate funding options that align with its strategic objectives and enhance long-term shareholder value," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HLIB sees stronger sales momentum for MCE on new model launches
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) anticipates a significant boost in MCE Holdings Bhd's sales in the second half, supported by several key factors.
The firm noted that MCE will see a full-quarter contribution from the new Proton Saga model starting in the third quarter of financial year 2026 (Q3 FY26), after minimal impact in the first half due to the phase-out of the legacy model in the first quarter.
"While the value per car remains broadly unchanged, stronger demand for the new model should translate into higher revenue contribution.
"Secondly, MCE will also see a full-quarter contribution from Perodua Traz from Q3 FY26 onwards. As this is a new model, this represents a new income stream for the group," it said.
HLIB also expects a notable increase in QV-E supply from the end of Q3 FY26, following slower-than-anticipated sales of the QV-E model.
Additionally, higher shipments to its US customer, Dorman, together with growing contributions from the non-automotive segment, are expected to further boost revenue.
The firm noted that looking ahead, the group is poised to gain its first contribution from a new US customer, JVIS, beginning in Q1 FY27.
"Beyond the factors highlighted above, demand could be further supported by vehicle scrapping incentives for cars aged over 20 years, which should lift replacement demand for Proton and Perodua models, providing an additional tailwind to MCE's volume growth.
"On the cost front, depreciation from the new plant is expected to commence from 3Q onwards, which we estimate at RM1.3 million per annum," it added.
HLIB has maintained its "Buy" rating on MCE, keeping the target price at RM2.38.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 850kg of subsidised cooking oil seized in Kelantan raid, three arrested
Article:
PASIR MAS: Enforcement officers have seized 850kg of subsidised cooking oil worth RM2,125 in a raid on a house in Kampung Lubuk Tapah, Kelantan.
The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry said three local men were arrested during the operation, which was carried out by its intelligence and special operations unit about 3.45pm yesterday.
The suspects were detained while transferring boxes of cooking oil into the house, which is believed to have been used as a transit point.
Authorities said they had earlier tracked a white Toyota Hiace van, suspected of being used by a syndicate to transport the shipment to the location.
Officers seized the cooking oil and the van.
An electricity bill linked to the premises was also seized as evidence.
The case is being investigated under the Control of Supplies Act 1961.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Receding Pedu Dam reveals submerged 50-year-old ancestral graves
Article:
PADANG TERAP: The drought causing water levels at the Pedu Dam to recede has once again revealed a submerged village and its ancestral cemetery, drawing residents to pay their respects.
The area, formerly known as Kampung Mong Gajah, was among the settlements inundated during the dam's construction around 1974.
Whenever the water recedes to expose the old burial grounds, descendants from Kampung Pinang, Kampung Tong Pelu, and Kampung Pedu gather to hold tahlil prayers for their relatives.
A survey of the site found that areas usually deep underwater have been transformed into vast open fields, attracting crowds and fishing enthusiasts.
[[nid:1404645]]
Angler Zakaria Abdul Hamid, 63, said the receding water had exposed the graves, similar to a dry spell two years ago.
"Before this, the graves were hidden, but now they are visible. It creates a very different atmosphere. Many people stay overnight here, usually arriving after Asar prayers," he said.
Zakaria, who has been fishing at the dam for five years after discovering the spot on Facebook, noted the unique transition of the landscape.
Another angler, Rashid Yusuf, 46, from Kampung Chong Sik, said the current water levels were significantly lower than earlier this year.
"When I came before Ramadan, the water had not receded like this. Now, so much land has emerged. I come here to fish for toman, so we camp and spend the night," he said.
Data from the Muda Agricultural Development Authority (Mada) website showed that the Muda Dam level continued to drop, reaching 7.84 per cent capacity yesterday.
The Pedu Dam recorded a level of 39.81 per cent, while the Ahning Dam stood at 54.21 per cent, bringing the total reservoir storage to 39.17 per cent.
[[nid:1404644]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump bars firms with pro-diversity policies from govt contracts
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump issued an executive order Thursday prohibiting companies doing business with the federal government from having policies meant to combat racism or sexism in the workforce.
The order, to come into effect in 30 days, is Trump's latest move against diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) policies, a series of often decades-old measures meant to combat racism and sexism across the United States.
The Republican president and many of his hard-right supporters say they believe diversity measures keep capable Americans out of jobs that instead go to minorities.
"My Administration has made significant progress in ending racial discrimination in American society, including so-called "diversity, equity, and inclusion" (DEI) activities," Trump wrote in the order.
He claimed that DEI policies also raise costs for employers, who then pass those on to the federal government when bidding for contracts.
Companies now hoping for government contracts will have to include on their contracts a seven-paragraph clause pledging that "The contractor will not engage in any racially discriminatory DEI activities."
Shortly after re-entering the White House for a second term last January, Trump put US federal employees working in diversity offices on paid leave after ordering the programs shut.
In August, his administration said it would "review" certain displays and exhibitions at the Smithsonian museums in Washington, looking to remove "divisive or partisan narratives" as part of his war on "woke" racial and gender viewpoints. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry: No wrongdoing in fishermen fuel purchase
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry has cleared a petrol station in Penaga of any wrongdoing following a viral video alleging suspicious fuel purchases.
Penang ministry director S. Jegan said enforcement officers conducted an inspection and confirmed the Shell station is licensed by the Malaysian Fisheries Development Authority (LKIM) to supply subsidised diesel to fishermen under the e-Nelayan card system.
He said the transaction captured in the video involved four individuals, each of whom is a legitimate e-Nelayan cardholder.
"Based on our checks, each of the four is a legitimate e-Nelayan cardholder.
"As such, no offence has been committed," he told the New Straits Times today.
An 18-second video has gone viral showing several man filling petrol into plastic cans placed in their boot.
Jegan explained that fishermen must be physically present to scan their personal details before any fuel purchase can be made, ensuring proper verification under the subsidised diesel programme.
"This was further confirmed through receipts checked during the inspection carried out on March 25 about 9pm," he added.
He also reminded the public not to make unverified accusations on social media, urging those with information or complaints to report directly to the ministry.
"Making baseless allegations online can expose individuals to defamation," he said.
Three days ago, a similar video went viral showing a man filling a plastic container with RON95 petrol at a station on Jalan Tengah, Bayan Baru.
The plastic container was placed in the boot of his car.
The ministry confirmed that no offence was committed in the viral incident.
Jegan had said that the man seen in the video is a fisherman with a valid permit to purchase fuel in accordance with existing laws, and subject to a stipulated quota.
His fuel purchase complied with regulations and did not involve any wrongdoing.

The man has threatened to initiate legal action against those who spread the video.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 2023 heat guidelines sufficient for schools, says Education Ministry
Article:
GOPENG: The existing guidelines on managing schools during hot weather are sufficient to safeguard students, Deputy Education Minister Wong Kah Woh said.
He said the guidelines, introduced in 2023, already provide clear direction to ensure students' safety and comfort during periods of high temperatures, with monitoring by schools and district education offices.
Wong added that decisions on additional measures, including steps to minimise students' exposure to extreme heat, are best left to school administrators such as principals and headteachers.
"School leaders have the experience and judgment to advise students appropriately based on the situation," he said.
"Regardless of the weather, staying hydrated is already a common practice. I believe the current measures are adequate."
He reiterated that the Education Ministry has no plans to make it compulsory for students to bring their own water bottles to school, despite ongoing hot weather linked to the El Niño phenomenon.
His remarks came during a working visit to Sekolah Jenis Kebangsaan Tamil Gopeng, where he inspected an upgrade project for dilapidated school buildings.
They were in response to calls by the Consumers Association of Penang, whose education officer N.V. Subbarow had urged the ministry to mandate water bottles for students.
Subbarow also proposed that the government provide free, plastic-free water bottles to students from low-income families under a Rahmah initiative.
Wong said schools are already guided on how to respond to hot weather conditions.
Under the existing framework, administrators may temporarily close schools if temperatures exceed 37°C for three consecutive days, classified as a Level Two heat alert.
If temperatures range between 35°C and 37°C for three consecutive days, all outdoor activities — including sports, camping and co-curricular programmes — must be suspended.
He added that education authorities at all levels have sufficient experience in managing such seasonal conditions, which occur annually.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Redouble efforts for a resilient and diversified energy future
Article:
SINCE the start of the Middle East war by the United States and Israel against Iran, the world has faced an energy crunch the likes of which was last seen in the 1970s.
There is nothing like an energy crisis to grip global attention as a spike in oil prices affects just about everybody, everywhere.
Malaysia has been thankfully relatively insulated, as we still enjoy highly subsidised prices at the pump each time we go to the petrol kiosk, and there are none of the long queues that seem to appear overnight in many countries, including neighbouring ones.
I pumped a full tank just before Hari Raya, with the bill exceeding RM150.
But I was grateful, of course, that I only paid roughly two-thirds of that bill out of my own pocket.
Still, there is no room for any complacency even if Malaysia is a net energy exporter.
The peculiarities of energy infrastructure are such that we cannot simply switch from importing for our energy needs to substituting with our exports.
Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Energy Transition and Water Transformation Datuk Amar Fadillah Yusof has been quick to come up with practical advice on how households can cut back on the use of energy and water supplies.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has also just followed up with the sensible precaution of cutting back on the quota limit for subsidised petrol while retaining the subsidies at existing levels.
It is also eminently sensible that we have, as a matter of official policy, maintain a healthy mix of electricity generation from various sources, even as we seek to cut back on fossil fuels.
Many Asian countries, heavily reliant on energy imports, have lately scrambled to fall back on coal as an energy source.
Energy, like food, is an essential commodity, and we source it every which way we can in times of an emergency like now.
Sarawak has been particularly blessed as it has, over the years of investment in dam-building, built up a substantial stock of renewable energy.
Less well-known is the fact that Borneo, including Sarawak, is one of those rare islands in Southeast Asia that holds significant reserves of coal deposits.
As a result of Sarawak's aspirations to be a provider of mostly "clean" energy, previous exports of Sarawak coal are now banned.
But the ban has now exempted the supply of Sarawak coal to Peninsular Malaysia, where Tenaga Nasional Berhad's coal-fired power plants still supply an estimated 50-per cent of the peninsula's energy mix.
It is undoubtedly a no-brainer that if Sarawak can contribute to the national requirement for coal, we will be able to save much foreign exchange through reduced coal imports.
As the world grapples with this latest energy crisis and hopes the Iran war will end soon, so the global energy shortages can gradually recover, Malaysia must redouble its efforts to maintain a healthy energy mix even as we seek ways to reduce reliance on traditional energy sources.
We must also learn to become less dependent on fuel subsidies by economising wherever possible, so the subsidies can gradually be phased out.
The billions spent annually on such subsidies can be better utilised for other developmental needs.
* The writer views developments in the nation, region and the wider world from his vantage point in Kuching
*The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Security guard with HIV, leukaemia jailed 10 years for raping disabled woman
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A security guard suffering from HIV and leukaemia was sentenced to 10 years' jail and five strokes of the cane by the Sessions Court today for raping a disabled woman in January.
Judge Madihah Zainol handed down the sentence after the accused, R. Sivanesan, 39, pleaded guilty to the charge.
According to the charge sheet, the man raped a 29-year-old woman whom he knew had physical and mental disabilities, including hearing and speech impairments and learning difficulties.
The offence was committed at a house in Jalan Jaya Mas 9, Taman Jaya Mas, Skudai, about 11.50pm on Jan 30.
He was charged under Section 376(2)(k) of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum jail term of 30 years and mandatory whipping.
Deputy public prosecutor Nor Fadilah Johanuddin appeared for the prosecution, while the accused was represented pro bono by lawyer Omar Kutty Abdul Aziz.
Earlier, Omar Kutty told the court that the accused was remorseful and sought forgiveness for his actions.
"The accused earns RM2,200 a month. He suffers from HIV and leukaemia, which requires him to make frequent hospital visits for treatment. He has also performed charity work at temples and has no prior criminal record," he said.
In pressing for a deterrent sentence, Nor Fadilah urged the court to prioritise public interest and the welfare of the victim, who is a person with disabilities.
"The accused took advantage of a victim with multiple disabilities. Furthermore, the fact that he has HIV means his actions exposed the victim to the risk of infectious venereal diseases," she said.
She requested a sentence that would serve as a lesson, along with police supervision and rehabilitation counselling.
The court ordered the accused to undergo rehabilitation counselling during his imprisonment and placed him under police supervision for one year upon his release.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sarawak fire department warns of sharp spike in open burning cases
Article:
SIBU: The Sarawak Fire and Rescue Department has urged the public to stop open burning following a sharp increase in fires across the state during the last two weeks of the month.
Its deputy director Tiong Ling Hii said the latest statistics showed a worrying upward trend, particularly between March 22 and 25.
"During the period from March 15 to 26, we saw a fluctuating pattern initially, but there was a significant spike starting on March 22. The peak was on March 25 with 35 cases recorded in a day, which was a very high level," he said in a statement today.
Tiong said most cases involved agricultural clearing and deliberate burning.
He noted that while the number of cases decreased slightly to 24 on March 26, the situation remained at a "cautious level" and required immediate preventive measures.
Miri was identified as the most critical division with 59 open fire cases, nearly double the 29 cases recorded in Sibu.
Other affected areas included Bintulu (24 cases), Mukah (23), and Kuching (11), followed by Betong (eight) and Sri Aman (five).
Serian, Samarahan, Sarikei, and Kapit each recorded between three and four cases, while Limbang reported no incidents during the period.
Tiong stressed that open burning during hot and dry weather allowed fires to spread rapidly and severely impacted air quality.
"The public is advised not to carry out any open burning, including burning garbage or clearing land. Strict action may be taken against individuals who violate this prohibition," he said.
He urged the community to report any fire incidents immediately to ensure prompt response.
"Cooperation from all parties is vital to prevent the situation from escalating and to protect public safety and the environment," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Election Court upholds Ruddy Awah's victory in Pitas state seat
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Election Court has struck out a petition filed by Warisan candidate Datuk Harun Ismail, challenging the results of the Pitas state seat won by Gabungan Rakyat Sabah's (GRS) Datuk Seri Ruddy Awah.
Kuching-based High Court judge Wong Siong Tung, sitting as the election court judge, made the decision via Zoom today after allowing preliminary objections raised by Ruddy and two other respondents.
"The petitioner, Harun, is ordered to pay RM15,000 in costs to Ruddy and a further RM15,000 to the second and third respondents," said lawyer Shahlan Jufri when contacted.
Harun, 66, filed the election petition on Dec 23 last year, naming Ruddy, 57, as the first respondent, the returning officer for the Pitas constituency as the second, and the Election Commission as the third respondent.
In the Sabah General Election, Ruddy, who is now the State Assistant Works and Utilities minister, secured the Pitas seat in a six-cornered contest with 5,754 votes and a majority of 1,565.
Harun received 4,189 votes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Newborn's body found near telecommunications substation in Kuala Pilah
Article:
KUALA PILAH: The body of a newborn baby boy, with his umbilical cord still attached, was found near a telecommunications substation near Masjid Rembang Panas here yesterday afternoon.
Kuala Pilah deputy police chief Deputy Superintendent Shamrol Shah Ramli said police received a report from a local man regarding the discovery at 6.30pm.
"Police are investigating the discovery of the unidentified infant found near the substation. A formal report was lodged at 6.54pm for further action," he said in a statement today.
Following the report, a team from the Kuala Pilah district police headquarters and the Negri Sembilan forensic division (D10) was deployed to the scene.
"Initial examinations revealed the body was a fully formed baby boy with the umbilical cord still intact. Further inspection suggested the infant had been dead for about two days and had begun to decompose," Shamrol said.
He added that no external injuries were found on the body. The infant's identity and the cause of death remain under investigation.
The case is being investigated under Section 318 of the Penal Code for concealment of birth by secret disposal of a dead body.
The remains were sent to the Tuanku Ampuan Najihah Hospital for a post-mortem.
Shamrol said police would also examine medical treatment records as part of the ongoing investigation.
Those with information can contact the Kuala Pilah police headquarters at 06-4842999 or the investigating officer, Inspector Supiah Ishak, at 019-9933978.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dry spell drives villagers to Golok River for daily needs
Article:
RANTAU PANJANG: Villagers along the Malaysia-Thai border are turning to Sungai Golok to bathe and carry out daily chores as water levels continue to fall following a prolonged dry spell.
Families, including children, were seen gathering along the riverbanks as early as 9am on Friday, bringing pails, soap, and laundry to make use of the accessible water source.
For many residents, the river has become a temporary alternative amid dwindling well water levels and reduced domestic supply.
"We come here when we have time, especially when the water level is lower, as it is easier and safer for children to bathe," said villager Aida Mat Zain.
The 40-year-old housewife said she brought her two children to the riverbank and even held a picnic there.
Some residents described the activity as an opportunity for communal bonding despite the challenges brought on by the dry conditions.
However, they urged authorities to continue monitoring the situation closely.
Meanwhile, Kelantan Department of Irrigation and Drainage director Dr Siti Fairus Zakaria said the water level at Sungai Golok in Rantau Panjang was recorded at 3.23m yesterday, which is 1.77m below its normal level of 5m.
She said the drop is consistent with the ongoing dry spell affecting several parts of the state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Senior citizen loses RM1 million to 'Singaporean' love scammer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 69-year-old woman from Penang has reportedly lost more than RM1 million after falling victim to a love scam involving a man posing as a Singaporean engineer based in the United Kingdom.
The case was highlighted by social media activist Kuan Chee Heng, popularly known as Uncle Kentang, after the victim approached him to borrow RM1,100 for her "lover" despite having already exhausted her savings.
Kuan said he had to refuse the woman's request for the loan after realising she was still unaware that she was being cheated.
"The victim reportedly met the suspect online. He claimed to be a Singaporean working as an engineer for a consultancy firm in the UK," Kuan said.
He said the woman fell in love with the suspect, who claimed he planned to move to Malaysia to live with her.
"The suspect also showed her a local bank fixed deposit document, believed to be forged, to gain her trust," he said when contacted.
Kuan said the victim had lived alone for a long time and was hoping for companionship in her old age.
Despite never meeting him in person, she fully trusted the man and repeatedly transferred money into several bank accounts provided to her, resulting in losses exceeding RM1 million, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM13mil floating mosque in Yan set for completion next month
Article:
YAN: The floating mosque in Kampung Ruat, scheduled for completion next month, is set to become one of the district's main attractions.
Deputy Works Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Maslan said the project, taken over by a new contractor in July 2023, involved a total construction cost exceeding RM12.95 million.
"The Public Works Department was appointed as the implementing agency for the project. The construction of this mosque is part of the government's initiative to provide safe and comfortable worship facilities for the local community.
"In addition, the mosque has the potential to emerge as a new tourism attraction for the state," he said after inspecting the project's progress here today.
Ahmad said the main scope of work included the construction of the mosque building and supporting structures such as an arch, water tank tower, azan tower, a compact Tenaga Nasional Bhd substation, and a Small Sewage Treatment System.
He said the handover to the Kedah Islamic Religious Council (Maik) was expected at the end of next month, after which the mosque would be opened to the public.
"The Works Ministry hopes that, upon completion, the floating mosque will serve not only as a house of worship but also as a symbol of development and community well-being in the Yan district," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Election court strikes out Karambunai petition, PAS's Aliakbar retains seat
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Election Court has struck out a petition seeking to nullify the result of the Karambunai state seat in the 17th Sabah general election.
PAS candidate Datuk Dr Aliakbar Gulasan remains the Karambunai assemblyman.
Kuching-based High Court judge Wong Siong Tung, sitting as the election court judge, delivered the decision via Zoom after allowing all preliminary objections raised by the respondents, including Aliakbar.
"The court finds that the petition is fundamentally defective and cannot proceed. Accordingly, the petition is struck out.
"The petitioner is ordered to pay costs in the sum of RM15,000 to the first and second respondents, and a further RM15,000 to the third respondent (Aliakbar)," said lawyer Rizwandean Bukhary M Burhan, who represented Aliakbar together with Shahlan Jufri.
The petition was filed on Dec 29, 2025 by Warisan candidate Ahmad Jais Otong, who named the returning officer (RO) for the Karambunai constituency, the Election Commission (EC), and Aliakbar as the first, second, and third respondents, respectively.
The respondents raised preliminary objections to strike out the petition, including on the grounds that it failed to demonstrate how any non-compliance with election rules and regulations affected the outcome.
The 11-cornered contest saw Aliakbar, contesting under the Perikatan Nasional ticket, secure 7,054 votes to win with a slim majority of 365 votes, defeating Ahmad (6,689), as well as candidates from Gabungan Rakyat Sabah (4,818) and Barisan Nasional (4,475).
The RO and EC were represented by Senior Federal Counsel Shamsul Bolhassan, along with Federal Counsels Safiyyah Omar, Nur Akmal Mohamad Azri and Nurul Muhaimin Mohd Azman.
The petitioner was represented by lawyers Chin Yean Yean and Cason Chin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syndicate misappropriating RON95 petrol crippled near Gerik
Article:
IPOH: The Perak Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry has crippled a syndicate involved in the misappropriation of subsidised RON95 petrol following the arrest of three men in Pengkalan Hulu, near Gerik, yesterday.
Its director, Kamalludin Ismail, said the operation, codenamed Ops Tiris, was carried out in collaboration with the Road Transport Department (RTD).
The operation targeted vehicles suspected of being modified to store fuel.
"Initial investigations revealed that the syndicate's modus operandi was to repeatedly refuel at several nearby petrol stations before heading towards a neighbouring country," he said in a statement.
The three local suspects, aged between 60 and 70, were detained to assist with the investigation.
Authorities also seized three vehicles believed to have modified fuel tanks, along with 450 litres of RON95 petrol, with a total estimated value of RM15,741.50.
The case is being investigated under the Control of Supplies Act 1961 for suspected possession or storage of controlled goods without written permission from the ministry's supply controller.
Kamalludin stressed that the ministry would take firm action against those involved in the misuse of subsidised goods, including prosecution in court.
"The ministry will continue to intensify enforcement through the Kita Gempur initiative as a strategic measure to combat the diversion of controlled goods and other forms of business manipulation," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three Thai nationals nabbed smuggling petrol at Rantau Panjang ICQS
Article:
RANTAU PANJANG: Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry enforcement officers detained three Thai nationals for attempting to smuggle petrol into Thailand today.
The suspects, two men and a woman, were intercepted at the Rantau Panjang Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) complex at 7.30am during Ops Tiris.
The ministry seized three Proton Waja cars and fuel with a combined value of RM15,619.20.
In the first case, a 53-year-old man was detained with 50 litres of petrol, with the total seizure valued at RM5,193.50.
In the second case, a 56-year-old man was caught with 60 litres of petrol and a vehicle worth RM5,232.20.
The third case involved a 35-year-old woman found with 50 litres of petrol and a car valued at RM5,193.50.
Initial investigations revealed that all three suspects admitted the petrol was intended for sale in the neighbouring country.
The cases are being investigated under Section 21 of the Control of Supplies Act 1961, read together with Section 8(4)(a) of the same act, for possession of controlled goods with intent to misappropriate.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadillah: Govt assessing nuclear energy potential to boost long-term security
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia is undertaking a comprehensive assessment of its potential nuclear energy programme, covering policy development, regulatory frameworks, project feasibility, industry participation, stakeholder engagement and human capital development.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said the move to explore nuclear energy for electricity generation is a strategic effort to strengthen long-term energy security while supporting the country's clean energy transition.
He said the initiative aligns with the Thirteenth Malaysia Plan (13MP), tabled by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim on July 31, 2025.
"The need to evaluate the feasibility of nuclear energy is increasingly relevant amid a changing global energy landscape shaped by geopolitical uncertainties and fluctuations in fuel supply and prices," he said in a statement.
MyPower Corporation Malaysia, an agency under the Energy Transition and Water Transformation Ministry (Petra), has been appointed as the Nuclear Energy Programme Implementing Organisation (Nepio), tasked with coordinating preparations based on the phased approach recommended by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA).
Fadillah said global tensions — particularly in the Middle East — and disruptions to key energy routes such as the Strait of Hormuz have affected energy markets and supply stability.
"In this context, nuclear energy offers stable, low-carbon baseload power and can reduce dependence on fossil fuels," he said.
He added that several Asean countries are also revisiting nuclear energy as part of their diversification strategies.
The Philippines is targeting up to 4,800 megawatts of nuclear capacity by 2050, while Vietnam has reintroduced nuclear power into its national energy plan. Indonesia is exploring small modular reactors (SMRs), including floating reactors, with a target timeline around 2030.
Fadillah said Malaysia's priority is to ensure any future nuclear development is carefully evaluated through comprehensive technical analysis and aligned with national priorities and international obligations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two Penang company directors lose RM890,000 to phone, investment scams
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Two company directors in Penang have lost a combined RM890,837.91 after falling victim to separate phone and investment scams.
The victims, a 69-year-old woman and a 47-year-old man, were duped by suspects posing as government authorities and business associates.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the cases, reported on March 26, were being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating.
He said in the first case, a 69-year-old female company director lost RM457,607 after being deceived by individuals impersonating officials from the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) and the police.
"The victim received a call on March 1 while at her home in Simpang Ampat. The caller claimed her bank account was linked to a criminal case and instructed her to lodge an online police report before connecting her to another suspect posing as a Bukit Aman officer.
"The victim was then accused of involvement in money laundering and drug trafficking, and told an arrest warrant had been issued against her. She was instructed to hand over her banking details and withdraw her savings for 'investigation purposes'.
"She made two cash handovers — RM168,477 on March 11 and RM289,130 on March 13 — to unknown individuals in Taman Tambun Indah," he said today.
Azizee said the suspects continued to demand more money, prompting the victim to realise she had been cheated.
In a separate case, a 47-year-old male company director lost RM433,230.91 after falling victim to a scam involving a fake investment offer.
He said the victim received a message on March 24 from a suspect impersonating a known foreign business associate, offering an opportunity to invest in a company acquisition in Hong Kong.
"The victim was later contacted via WhatsApp and email by another suspect posing as a lawyer from a London-based firm.
"Believing the deal to be genuine, he transferred RM433,230.91 to a Hong Kong bank account on the same day," he added.
Azizee said the victim later verified the offer with the actual individual and discovered the transaction was fraudulent.
He urged the public to remain vigilant against suspicious calls and messages, especially those requesting personal or banking details or offering lucrative investments.
He said checks on suspicious accounts could be made via the Semak Mule portal, while victims should immediately contact the National Scam Response Centre (NSRC) hotline at 997.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MEF calls for proactive fuel conservation, sustainable energy usage amid current uncertainties
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: THE Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) emphasises that all segments of society must play a proactive and responsible role in conserving fuel and ensuring sustainable energy usage amid ongoing uncertainties in the Middle East.
Its president Datuk Dr Syed Hussain Syed Husman said the evolving geopolitical developments have heightened volatility in global energy markets, but Malaysia cannot remain insulated from these external pressures.
"MEF fully supports the government's fuel rationalisation efforts and calls on all stakeholders to act decisively and responsibly in managing fuel consumption," he said in a statement today.
He also noted that fuel rationalisation must be approached as a shared national responsibility, and not a challenge for the government alone.
"It requires a whole-of-nation approach, where policymakers provide clear direction and support, businesses drive efficiency and innovation, and the rakyat embrace more responsible consumption habits," he said.
Syed Hussain said MEF remains committed to working closely with the government to ensure that fuel rationalisation measures are implemented in a manner that balances economic sustainability, business continuity, and national resilience.
Looking ahead, MEF also encourages businesses to accelerate their transition towards sustainable practices and alternative energy solutions, he said.
"Employers should begin investing in renewable energy, including solar solutions, and explore the gradual adoption of electric vehicles where feasible.
"Establishing internal task forces to identify fuel-saving strategies across operations will also be critical," he added.
In a special address yesterday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced that, effective April 1, the individual monthly limit for the purchase of  Budi95 will be temporarily adjusted to 200 litres per month from 300 litres per month previously, due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
A majority of the rakyat are not affected by the quota reduction, as consumers, on average, use about 100 litres per month, and nearly 90 per cent consume less than 200 litres per month.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Post-Hari Raya: When family homes fall quiet
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As Aidilfitri ends, the warmth and bustle of family homes give way to quiet emptiness, leaving many to face the stillness of returning to daily routines.
A TikTok
video
posted by @manchong captured the poignant moment, showing the calm of his house as family members departed.
Last week, families celebrated Hari Raya, and this week many have begun returning home.
He later wrote in the comments that he was the youngest in the family, offering encouragement to others in similar situations.
The
video
struck a chord with many Malaysians, who shared their own feelings of post-festive emptiness.
One user, @e., wrote: "This year Raya felt the shortest. By the second and third day, we had to work, and by the fourth day everyone had returned home. It's okay, I'm used to it."
Another, @azz, said: "The saddest part is walking through the house and feeling like they are still there when they have already left. It's not nice being the youngest."
A more emotional response came from @2611, reflecting on parents' loneliness: "Mum feels the silence most because she loves welcoming guests. Now, with her children grown and working, she is often alone. I hope to get a permanent job so I can spend more time with her and care for her as she cared for us."
The clip highlighted a shared reality: while Aidilfitri brings joy and togetherness, its end often leaves a quiet sadness in empty homes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CIMB to develop Islamic financial instrument under SC innovation lab
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: CIMB Holdings Bhd (CIMB) has announced plans to develop an innovative financial instrument aimed at strengthening Malaysia's Islamic capital markets, in line with Securities Commission Malaysia's (SC) Capital Market Masterplan 2026-2030.
The initiative will be carried out in collaboration with SC and Bursa Malaysia Bhd under a pilot project within SC's ICM Innovation Lab (FIKRALab).
The proposed instrument is designed to expand investment opportunities for Syariah-compliant funds, supporting the growth of Asean's Islamic banking sector.
CIMB said it is entering this phase from a position of strength, with Islamic finance forming a key pillar of its growth strategy.
More than half of the group's Malaysian financing portfolio is Syariah-compliant, while its Indonesian subsidiary, CIMB Niaga, is in the process of refurbishing its Islamic banking operations into a full-fledged Islamic bank.
CIMB group chief executive officer Novan Amirudin said the collaboration reflects CIMB's commitment to innovation and its support for SC's long-term vision for the capital market.
"Our Islamic franchise has reached meaningful scale, and we see significant opportunity to further unlock this value by providing new Syariah-compliant structures to investors with a Shariah mandate," he said.
CIMB's role in the pilot project highlights its contribution to shaping the next phase of Malaysia's capital market development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE AM MARCH 27, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Friday, outperforming regional peers as market sentiment improved following the government's adjusted fuel subsidy measures.
The move supported fiscal stability and eased cost pressures on households amid ongoing West Asia uncertainties.
For today, the index is expected to remain range-bound between 1,710 and 1,730.
In the crypto market, Bitcoin rose to RM275,750.
Ethereum rose to RM8,289, while Solana was up at RM347.
That's it for Market Pulse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RHB sees 19pct earnings growth for Binastra, keeps "Buy" call
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: RHB Research has projected a three-year earnings compound annual growth rate of 19 per cent for Binastra Corp Bhd from financial year 2026 to financial year 2029 (FY26-FY29).
The firm said this is premised on higher progress billings from its RM7.1 billion balance orderbook, which translates into an orderbook-to-revenue cover ratio of 4.7 times.
"Growth prospects are strengthened further by Binastra's venture into data centre and renewable energy (RE) projects," it said in a note.
The firm said it has kept its earnings forecasts unchanged as Binastra's results met expectations while introducing its FY29 earnings projections, which factor in a new job win target of RM2.5 billion.
"Looking ahead, Binastra is targeting to secure over RM2 billion in new jobs in FY27, while our internal replenishment target stands higher at RM3 billion with year-to-date FY27 job wins standing at RM819.5 million.
"After making a blockbuster debut down south during FY26 – by clinching RM2.5 billion worth of contracts in Johor Bahru – Binastra's target for new jobs would now focus more on data centres, RE, and some residential property jobs in Klang Valley," it added.
RHB Research said diesel costs currently account for about one per cent or less of Binastra's total cost structure.
It added that any unexpected increases in building material prices, such as steel and cement, can be managed through discussions with project owners to seek possible reimbursement.
The firm has reiterated its "Buy" rating on Binastra, keeping the target price unchanged at RM2.72.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Missing man last seen in Kota Damansara, police seek public help
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Police are seeking public assistance to locate a man reported missing in Kota Damansara.
Petaling Jaya district deputy police chief Superintendent M Hussin Sollehuddin Zolkifly said a report was lodged at 9.52am on Wednesday regarding the disappearance of Cheah Yew Wei, 33.
He said the man was last seen around 8pm on March 15 in the D'Rimba apartment area in Kota Damansara.
"Cheah is approximately 178cm tall, weighs about 100kg and has a fair complexion," he said in a statement.
Those with information are urged to contact investigating officer Sergeant Major Roszaidi Abdul Talib at 019-3778383 or the Petaling Jaya district police headquarters operations room at 03-79662222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lorry with modified tank leads police to 2,000-litre diesel stash in Kedah, suspect arrested
Article:
JITRA, March 27 — Police have arrested a man and seized 2,250 litres of subsidised diesel believed to have been misappropriated in an operation, codenamed Op Tiris, at a petrol station in Bukit Kayu Hitam last Wednesday.
Kubang Pasu police chief Mohd Radzi Abdul Rahim said the 32-year-old local man was detained at about 10am along with a lorry found in suspicious circumstances.
“Inspection of the vehicle uncovered several items linked to diesel misappropriation activities, including subsidy cards belonging to other companies and a modified additional tank containing 250 litres of fuel,” he said at a press conference.
Further investigations led police to a rented house in Kampung Padang Satu, where a storage area was discovered containing more than 2,000 litres of diesel kept in barrels and tanks.
Mohd Radzi said the syndicate used a tactic of concealing the modified tank under a wooden pallet to avoid detection by enforcement authorities.
“The suspect would refuel in stages, filling up at multiple petrol stations before transferring the fuel into storage containers.
“The diesel was then stored in barrels and tanks, with buyers, both local and from neighbouring countries, coming to purchase it, allowing the suspect to make higher profits,” he said.
He added that rising demand for fuel, including from neighbouring countries amid the West Asian crisis, has driven syndicates to adopt new smuggling methods.
The suspect, who has no prior criminal record, has been remanded for four days and will be handed over to the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living for further investigation under Section 21 of the Control of Suppliers Act 1961, read together with Regulation 3(1) of the Control of Supplies Regulations 1974.
Police are continuing efforts to trace the owners of the subsidy cards used in the operation.
Mohd Radzi also urged petrol station operators and the public to provide information on such activities, warning that those found abetting smuggling could face legal action. — Bernama

Title: Police: Bystander slashed in KLCC melee after stepping in to stop alleged assault on tourist, 18 ‘photo touts’ held
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 27 — Police have arrested 18 men following a violent altercation near Kuala Lumpur City Centre (KLCC) that began with an alleged assault on a foreign tourist and escalated into a chaotic group attack, Dang Wangi police chief Sazalee Adam said in a statement today.
The incident is believed to have occurred at about 9pm on March 22, when a man witnessed a tourist being beaten by a group of freelance photographers operating in the area.
When the man attempted to intervene, he was allegedly attacked from behind with a
kerambit
knife and subsequently assaulted by several individuals, with the situation quickly turning into a large-scale disturbance involving 30 people.
Police said the victim sustained injuries to the head.
Sazalee said all 18 suspects, aged between 17 and 45, were detained in an operation carried out in the vicinity of KLCC the following day.
“Initial investigations found that all the suspects admitted to being present at the scene and are believed to be working as freelance photographers, commonly referred to as ‘
ulat foto
’, around the KLCC area,” he said.
One of the suspects has a prior criminal record, while the majority do not, authorities said.
Investigations are ongoing, including efforts to establish the motive behind the attack and to identify other individuals who may have been involved in the incident.
Police have not disclosed further details on the condition or identity of the tourist.

Title: Impersonation scams leave two Penang company directors nearly RM900,000 poorer, say police
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, March 27 — Two company directors in Penang have lost a combined total of nearly RM900,000 after falling victim to separate phone scam syndicates involving impersonation tactics.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the first case involved a 69-year-old woman who lost RM457,607 after being deceived by individuals posing as officers from the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission and the police.
The victim, who was at her home in Simpang Ampat, received a call on March 1 from a man claiming her bank account was linked to a criminal case.
She was later connected to another suspect, impersonating a police officer from Bukit Aman, who accused her of involvement in money laundering and drug trafficking.
She was told an arrest warrant had been issued in her name and was instructed to cooperate by providing her banking details and withdrawing her savings for “investigation purposes,” with assurances the money would be returned.
On March 11, she handed over RM168,477 in cash to an unknown individual in Taman Tambun Indah, followed by another RM289,130 on March 13 to a different suspect in the same area.
“The suspects continued to demand more money, which raised the victim’s suspicions. She later realised she had been scammed and lodged a police report,” Azizee said.
In a separate case, a 47-year-old male company director lost RM433,230.91 after being duped by a scammer posing as a foreign business associate.
The victim received a message on March 24 from an international number claiming to be “Dag Rasmussen,” allegedly a major shareholder of a Norwegian company known to him. The suspect offered him an opportunity to invest in the acquisition of a company in Hong Kong.
The victim was then contacted via WhatsApp and email by another individual posing as a London-based lawyer, who provided documents for a supposed confidential agreement.
Believing the deal to be genuine, the victim transferred RM433,230.91 from his company account to a Hong Kong bank account on the same day.
He later became suspicious after conducting checks and confirmed with the real individual that no such transaction existed.
Both cases are being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating. — Bernama

Title: Ten years’ jail, five strokes for man who raped vulnerable PwD woman in Skudai
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, March 27 — A 39-year-old man living with HIV was sentenced to 10 years’ imprisonment and five strokes of the cane by the Sessions Court here today for raping a woman with disabilities last January.
Judge Madihah Zainol handed down the sentence after the accused, R. Sivanesan, pleaded guilty when the charge was read out by a court interpreter.
According to the charge sheet, the accused raped a 29-year-old woman, whom he knew had hearing and speech impairments as well as a learning disability, at the time of the incident.
The offence was committed at a house in Taman Jaya Mas in Skudai near here at about 11.50pm on January 30.
He was charged under Section 376(2)(k) of the Penal Code, which provides for up to 30 years’ imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
The provision covers aggravated rape, including cases where the perpetrator is aware of the victim’s mental or physical vulnerability.
The prosecution was led by Deputy Public Prosecutor Nor Fadilah Johanuddin, while the accused was represented by counsel Omar Kutty Abdul Aziz on a
pro bono
basis.
In mitigation, Omar Kutty told the court that the accused had apologised and was remorseful for his actions.
He added that the accused, who also suffers from leukaemia, earns RM2,200 a month as a security guard and has no prior criminal record.
In her submission, Nor Fadilah urged the court to consider the case as one of public interest and the victim’s suffering.
“The accused took advantage of the victim who has a physical disability, namely speech, hearing and learning disabilities.
“As the accused is HIV-positive, his actions had exposed the victim to the risk of sexually transmitted diseases.
“I request for a punishment that is appropriate and for the accused to be under police supervision and rehabilitative counselling,” she said.
The court imposed the prison sentence and caning, and ordered the accused to undergo rehabilitative counselling throughout his imprisonment. He will also be placed under police supervision for one year after his release.

Title: Saifuddin Nasution: Govt keeps RON95 at RM1.99 to shield Malaysians from rising costs
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, March 27 — The Madani government remains committed to protecting the people from global economic pressures by maintaining the subsidised price of RON95 petrol at RM1.99 per litre under the BUDI Madani RON95 (BUDI95) programme, keeping Malaysia among countries with the lowest fuel prices globally.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the move comes as global crude oil prices have surged beyond US$100 per barrel, affecting many countries.
He noted that petrol prices in some countries exceed RM10 per litre, including in Singapore, and reach about RM14 per litre in Hong Kong.
“However, in Malaysia, the Madani government under the leadership of Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has taken a clear stance — the people must be protected.
“The government understands the burden if these costs are passed on to the rakyat. Therefore, it has decided to remain a ‘shield’ for the people,” he said in a Facebook post today.
He said although the pump price remains at RM1.99, the actual cost borne by the government has risen sharply, with the fuel subsidy bill increasing sixfold from RM700 million to RM4 billion per month.
“As a ‘big family’, we must make wise choices. We cannot allow RM4 billion of public funds to be ‘burned’ on the roads every month.
“These subsidy savings are needed to build more schools, upgrade hospitals and provide direct cash assistance to those in need,” he said.
To ensure balance, he said the government will temporarily adjust the BUDI95 eligibility quota from 300 litres to 200 litres per month, effective April 1.
He added that the adjustment will not affect the majority of Malaysians, as data shows nearly 90 per cent consume an average of about 100 litres of petrol monthly.
“This means the daily lives of the vast majority (nine out of 10 people) will continue as usual without additional costs. E-hailing drivers also need not worry, as their quota remains at 800 litres per month to support their livelihood.
“For the 10 per cent who consume more than 200 litres monthly, I urge them to view this adjustment as a step towards a more sustainable lifestyle,” he said.
He added that as the country manages global economic challenges, higher-income and higher-spending groups are encouraged to be more prudent by reducing unnecessary travel, practising carpooling or using public transport.
He said the Madani government has absorbed global price increases to prevent a sharp rise in the cost of living.
“Let us be more prudent and responsible in managing our fuel consumption. This measure is temporary until global conditions stabilise.
“This reflects the strength of Malaysia Madani — while other countries pass the burden to their people, Malaysia chooses to protect them,” he added. — Bernama

Title: Newborn baby found dead near Kuala Pilah mosque, police investigate
Article:
SEREMBAN, March 27 — The body of a full-term baby boy, still with the umbilical cord attached, was found near a telecommunications tower substation close to Masjid Rembang Panas in Kuala Pilah yesterday.
Kuala Pilah District Police deputy chief DSP Shamrol Shah Ramli said police received a call from a local man at 6.30pm, reporting the discovery, before a formal report was lodged about half an hour later.
“Following that, police went to the scene, and an initial check found the baby had died about two days earlier and had begun to decompose.
“A physical examination of the victim did not reveal any external injuries, and the cause of death remains under investigation,” he said in a statement today.
The body was sent to Tuanku Ampuan Najihah Hospital for post-mortem, and the case is being investigated under Section 318 of the Penal Code.
Members of the public with information are urged to contact the Kuala Pilah Police Headquarters at 06-4842999 or the investigating officer, Insp Supiah Ishak, at 019-9933978 to assist with the investigation. — Bernama

Title: SUPP will resolve internal issues, says party leader amid speculation over Lily Yong’s sacking
Article:
KUCHING, March 27 — The Sarawak United People’s Party (SUPP) will be able to resolve any internal issues accordingly, said Datuk Wee Hong Seng.
The Kuching South mayor called for restraint and adherence to due process following speculation over SUPP Kuching chairman Datuk Lily Yong Lee Lee’s sacking.
“I’m not in the party’s Central Working Committee (CWC), so I don’t know what actually happened. If a person is not involved, then we should not comment to be fair.
“I’m pretty sure that a party such as SUPP with a long history will be able to resolve internal matters themselves. So let them do what should be done.
“There are a lot of rumours. It’s not healthy. Let’s focus on serving the people, especially amid global uncertainties and economic challenges,” he said when asked about Yong’s removal.
Responding to speculation linking the expulsion to past remarks on electoral candidacy, Wee dismissed such claims as longstanding rumours.
“These are rumours that have been around for many years. Why should we remember the past? We move on,” he added.
On allegations involving the Kenyalang Gold Card (KGC), he emphasised that any conclusions should be based on official findings.
“If that is the issue, then we must look at the findings. Like in court, we respect the judgment.
“If there is a process for appeal, then we must follow the procedure,” he said.
Yong was expelled by the party’s CWC along with SUPP Kuching secretary Voong Nam Jin.
SUPP Kuching has formed a special task force and appointed acting leaders, with deputy chairman Jong Yean Pin appointed acting chairman and Youth chief Nicholas Wung assuming the role of acting secretary in line with the party’s constitution.
According to Wung, the expulsion was communicated via a letter dated March 16, although it took effect from March 9, noting that details of the disciplinary findings have yet to be disclosed. — The Borneo Post

Title: MEF urges return to WFH, carpooling to combat Malaysia’s rising fuel costs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 27 — The Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) is urging businesses to implement work-from-home (WFH) arrangements for non-critical roles and encourage carpooling as immediate, practical steps to support the government’s new fuel rationalisation measures.
The call came as MEF expressed its full support for the government’s policy, which it described as a necessary response to the global energy crisis sparked by conflict in the Middle East.
MEF president Datuk Dr Syed Hussain Syed Husman said that since Malaysia cannot be insulated from these external pressures, managing fuel consumption must be a shared national responsibility.
“For the private sector, this includes implementing work-from-home arrangements for non-critical roles where feasible, encouraging carpooling initiatives, and promoting greater use of public transportation,” he said in a statement.
Beyond WFH, he called on businesses to optimise their logistics through better route management and to intensify the use of virtual meetings to cut down on physical travel.
Looking ahead, MEF also encouraged employers to accelerate their transition towards sustainable practices by investing in renewable energy and exploring the gradual adoption of electric vehicles.
While backing the government’s policy, Syed Hussain highlighted the concerns of businesses, particularly the impact of rising operational costs on logistics and manufacturing.
He warned that abrupt policy shifts could lead to supply chain disruptions and inflationary pressures that would ultimately be passed on to consumers, noting that small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) are especially vulnerable.
To mitigate these impacts, MEF called on the government to provide targeted support, such as tax incentives for energy-efficient investments, and to ensure a phased, pragmatic implementation that is sensitive to the realities of different industries.
Syed Hussain reiterated that the challenge requires a “whole-of-nation approach,” where policymakers provide clear direction, businesses drive efficiency, and the public embraces more responsible consumption habits.

Title: Sarawak Bomba calls for immediate halt to open burning as fire cases spike in March
Article:
SIBU, March 27 — The Sarawak Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) has urged the public to stop the practice of open burning following a sharp increase in open fire cases in the state during mid to late March 2026.
Sarawak JBPM deputy director Tiong Ling Hii said the latest statistics showed a worrying upward trend, especially starting from March 22 to its peak on March 25.
“During the period from March 15 to 26, we saw a fluctuating pattern at the initial stage, but there was a significant spike starting March 22. The peak was on March 25 with 35 cases recorded in a day, which was at a very high level.
“Most of the cases occurred due to burning activities for agricultural purposes in addition to deliberate burning,” he said in a statement today.
According to him, although the number of cases decreased slightly to 24 cases on March 26, the situation is still at a cautious level and requires immediate preventive measures by all parties.
In terms of distribution by division, Miri was identified as the most critical area with 59 open fire cases recorded, almost double that of Sibu which recorded 29 cases.
In addition, Bintulu (24 cases) and Mukah (23 cases) also showed a moderate risk level that required close monitoring. Kuching recorded 11 cases, followed by Betong (eight cases) and Sri Aman (five cases).
Several other areas such as Serian, Samarahan, Sarikei and Kapit recorded lower numbers of cases, between three and four cases, while Limbang did not record any cases during the period.
Tiong stressed that open burning, especially in hot and dry weather conditions, can cause fires to spread quickly and affect air quality.
“The public is advised not to carry out any open burning, including burning garbage or clearing land. Strict action may be taken against individuals who violate this prohibition,” he said.
He also urged the local community to immediately report any fire incidents to the fire department so that prompt action can be taken.
He added that cooperation from all parties is very important to control this situation from becoming more serious, especially in an effort to protect public safety and the environment. — Bernama

Title: Multi-agency operation launched to contain 16-hectare bushfire near Sibu driving institute
Article:
SIBU, March 27 — An inter-agency operation has been launched on Friday to contain a bushfire near the Ngu Chung Beng Driving Institute, which has burned about 16 hectares of scrubland.
Fire and Rescue Department of Malaysia (Bomba) Sibu Zone 4 Chief, Andy Alie said the response has been divided into three sectors, A, B and C, with a total of 50 personnel mobilised to support Bomba from the Sibu Central station.
He said the operation strategy this morning required teams to cross a small stream before carrying out defensive and direct-attack methods to curb the fire’s spread.
“Sectoral arrangements and team deployments were completed at 9am, and suppression work has been ongoing since,” he said.
The operation involves personnel from Bomba, Rela, the Civil Defence Force (APM), the police (PDRM), the Department of Irrigation and Drainage (DID), and volunteer units.
DID Sibu also deployed an excavator to carry out clearing works for better access to the affected area.
Andy said an emergency call was first received on March 23, but initial responders found the site to be roughly 2km inland from the nearest access route, making immediate suppression impossible.
“We could not carry out firefighting earlier because the fire was too deep inside and there was no proper access. Only today, with support from multiple agencies and machinery clearing the path, were we finally able to enter and begin full suppression,” he said.
He added that monitoring will continue, with resource deployment adjusted based on fire behaviour and ground conditions. — The Borneo Post

Title: Motorcyclist dies after late-night crash involving fertiliser lorry in Mukah
Article:
MUKAH, March 27 — A 30-year-old motorcyclist was killed in a road accident at KM20 of Jalan Mukah-Balingian late Thursday night.
Mukah police chief Muhamad Rizal Alias said the fatal crash occurred at about 11pm on March 26 and involved a 25-tonne lorry carrying fertiliser and a motorcycle.
“The lorry, driven by a 36-year-old man, was travelling straight from the Jalan Metading junction towards Mukah town when the collision occurred with a motorcycle coming from the opposite direction,” he said in a statement on Friday.
He added that initial investigations found that the motorcyclist had suddenly entered the path of the lorry, leading to the impact.
“The victim, who hails from from Kampung Kuala Penipah in Mukahh, sustained severe injuries to the head, left leg and left arm and was pronounced dead at the scene by a medical officer from Mukah Hospital,” he said.
The lorry driver escaped unhurt.
Rizal said the case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
He urged witnesses to come forward to assist in the investigation by contacting the investigating officer, Insp Mohd Rahmat Jalimin, at 011-31627137 or by visiting the nearest police station.
He also advised road users to exercise caution when driving or riding, especially along dark stretches at night, to prevent accidents. — The Borneo Post

Title: Floating mosque in Yan set to open in April, tipped as key tourism draw, says Ahmad Maslan
Article:
YAN, March 27 — The floating mosque in Kampung Ruat here, the project scheduled for full completion next month, is set to become one of the district’s main attractions.
Deputy Works Minister Datuk Seri Ahmad Maslan said the project, taken over by a new contractor in July 2023, involves a total construction cost exceeding RM12.95 million.
“The Public Works Department (JKR) has been appointed as the implementing agency for this project. The construction of this mosque is part of the government’s initiative to provide safe and comfortable worship facilities for the local community.
“In addition, the mosque has the potential to emerge as a new tourism attraction for the state,” he said after inspecting the progress of the floating mosque project here today.
Ahmad said that the main scope of work includes the construction of the mosque building, as well as supporting structures such as an arch, water tank tower, azan tower, a compact Tenaga Nasional Berhad substation, and a Small Sewage Treatment System (SSTS).
He said the handover of the project from JKR to the Kedah Islamic Religious Council (MAIK) is expected to take place at the end of next month, after which the mosque will be opened fully to the public.
“The Ministry of Works hopes that, upon completion, the floating mosque will serve not only as a house of worship but also as a symbol of development and community well-being in the Yan district,” he added. — Bernama

Title: Too hot to work? Malaysia’s labour unions push for legal protection amid heat spell
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 27 — A coalition of trade unions is demanding that the government legally recognize extreme heat as a deadly occupational hazard and enforce mandatory work stoppages during severe heatwaves, arguing that current voluntary guidelines leave millions of outdoor workers unprotected.
The Building and Woodworkers’ International – Malaysia Liaison Council (BWI-MLC) issued the call today amid a prolonged heatwave in the country, with temperatures hitting 40°C in some northern states.
The Ministry of Health has already reported 15 cases of heat-related illnesses this year.
In a statement, the coalition argued that while the public is advised to stay indoors, this is not an option for workers in sectors like construction, agriculture, and p-hailing.
“They are the most vulnerable group to prolonged heat exposure, yet their plight remains glaringly absent from mainstream public health discussions,” BWI-MLC said.
The group commended the existing 2016 guidelines from the Department of Occupational Safety and Health (DOSH), but criticised these as being merely advisory and lacking legal enforceability, leaving compliance entirely up to employers.
To address this, BWI-MLC urged the government to table a comprehensive National Heat Action Plan with clear, enforceable directives.
This plan must include legally guaranteed compensation so workers are not penalised with wage cuts for safety-related work suspensions, the group said.
Citing laws in South Korea as a precedent, the group also called for legal protection for contractors against financial penalties for project delays caused by these essential stoppages.
The unions are als pushing for mandatory provisions for all outdoor workers, including access to free drinking water, shaded rest areas, and appropriate cooling personal protective equipment (PPE).
They pointed to the Philippines, where the government has issued a legally enforceable advisory on heat safety, as a model for Malaysia to follow.
“This crisis demands a coordinated, cross-ministerial response,” the group said, calling for active worker representation in formulating any new policies.
“We know the realities on the ground, and our voices are essential in drafting life-saving climate adaptations.”

Title: Syndicate siphoning RON95 petrol crippled in Perak enforcement operation, three arrested
Article:
IPOH, March 27 — The Perak enforcement division of the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living has crippled a syndicate involved in the misappropriation of subsidised RON95 petrol following the arrest of three local men in an operation in Pengkalan Hulu near Gerik yesterday.
Its director, Kamalludin Ismail, said the operation, codenamed Ops Tiris and carried out in collaboration with the Road Transport Department, targeted vehicles suspected of being modified for illegal fuel storage.
